invariant_definition: Non-duality. As a school-name, Advaita refers principally to Śaṅkara's lineage and its commentarial tradition. As a doctrinal position, *advaita* is the claim that *brahman* is non-dual — without internal differentiation and without external second. The term is also used by other traditions in modified form (*viśiṣṭa-advaita* — the qualified non-duality of Rāmānuja; *śuddha-advaita* — the pure non-duality of Vallabha; *acintya-bhedābheda* — which can be read as a form of non-duality through *acintya-śakti*). The user's position-brief explicitly affirms a *paramādvaita* — a non-duality of duality and non-duality — that integrates rather than refutes the qualified positions.

per_school.definition: *Brahman* is *eka eva advitīyam* (one only, without a second; Chāndogya 6.2.1). All apparent duality is *vyāvahārika*; *pāramārthika* truth is non-dual identity of *ātman* and *brahman*.

*Viśiṣṭa-advaita* — non-duality of the qualified. *Brahman* is one but is *viśiṣṭa* (qualified) by *cit* and *acit* as his body. The unity is real, the qualifications are real; this is genuinely non-dual because there is no second-substance over against *brahman*.
*Śuddha-advaita* — pure non-duality. The world is a real *avikṛta-pariṇāma* of *brahman*; *māyā* in the Advaita sense is rejected. Non-duality is preserved without illusion-doctrine.

translator_note: 'Non-duality' is the standard gloss. Note: *advaita* as a doctrinal modifier is more permissive than *advaita* as a school-name — the various qualified-advaitas are *advaita* in the structural sense (no second substance) without being *Advaita* in the Śaṅkara-school sense (numerical identity of *jīva* and *brahman*).
